class_schedule.topic: Overview of the U.S. Healthcare System
learning_objectives: Describe the structure of the U.S. healthcare delivery system
Identify major stakeholders including payers, providers, and regulators
Understand the flow of patients through the care continuum
readings: Jonas and Kovner's Health Care Delivery in the United States Ch. 1-2
CMS National Health Expenditure Data Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: The U.S. healthcare system is the most expensive in the world yet ranks poorly on many quality metrics. Understanding its structure is essential for anyone working in health informatics.
key_concepts: Healthcare delivery models: fee-for-service, managed care, value-based
Major stakeholders: CMS, private insurers, providers, patients
Care settings: primary, acute, post-acute, long-term, home health
Healthcare spending: $4.3 trillion annually (18% of GDP)
The triple aim: better care, better health, lower costs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive diagram of patient flow from primary care through specialist referral, hospitalization, and discharge

review.value: IHI framework: improving patient experience, improving population health, reducing per capita cost
Payment model where providers are reimbursed for each service rendered, incentivizing volume over value

learning_objectives: Explain the major health insurance programs (Medicare, Medicaid, commercial)
Understand reimbursement mechanisms and their impact on care delivery
Analyze the shift from volume-based to value-based payment
readings: Jonas and Kovner's Health Care Delivery Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: How healthcare is financed fundamentally shapes how it is delivered. Understanding payer dynamics is essential for designing informatics solutions that align with financial incentives.
key_concepts: Medicare Parts A, B, C, D and their coverage scopes
Medicaid and state-level variation
Commercial insurance and employer-sponsored plans
DRG-based payment and prospective payment systems
Value-based payment models: ACOs, bundled payments, MIPS
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of a CMS-1500 claim form and explanation of coding, billing, and reimbursement cycle

review.value: Diagnosis-Related Group: classification system grouping hospital cases into categories for fixed payment amounts
Accountable Care Organization: groups of providers who share responsibility and financial risk for a patient population

learning_objectives: Describe the role of primary care in population health
Understand the Patient-Centered Medical Home model
Identify informatics tools supporting ambulatory care
readings: Jonas and Kovner's Health Care Delivery Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: Primary care is the foundation of a high-functioning healthcare system. Informatics tools can help primary care practices manage populations, coordinate care, and improve outcomes.
key_concepts: Primary care workforce: physicians, NPs, PAs
Patient-Centered Medical Home (PCMH) principles
Practice management systems and scheduling
Patient portals and secure messaging
Population health management in primary care
lecture_notes.demonstration: Demo of a patient portal showing appointment scheduling, lab results, and secure messaging

review.value: Care model where a primary care provider coordinates all aspects of a patient's care including specialists and community services
Secure web application allowing patients to access their health records, communicate with providers, and manage appointments

learning_objectives: Describe the organization and operations of hospital systems
Understand clinical workflows in inpatient settings
Identify key informatics systems used in hospitals
readings: Jonas and Kovner's Health Care Delivery Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: Hospitals are the most complex organizations in healthcare. Their clinical and operational workflows demand sophisticated informatics solutions.
key_concepts: Hospital organizational structure and governance
Inpatient clinical workflow: admission, treatment, discharge
CPOE (Computerized Provider Order Entry) systems
Medication administration and barcode scanning
Clinical documentation and nursing informatics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulated inpatient workflow showing order entry, pharmacy verification, medication administration, and documentation

review.value: System where providers enter orders electronically, enabling clinical decision support and reducing medication errors
Right patient, right drug, right dose, right route, right time - verified by barcode medication administration

learning_objectives: Explain quality measurement frameworks in healthcare
Understand the role of informatics in quality reporting
Analyze the transition to value-based care models
readings: Jonas and Kovner's Health Care Delivery Ch. 12

lecture_notes.introduction: Measuring and improving quality is central to modern healthcare. Informatics professionals build the systems that capture, compute, and report quality metrics.
key_concepts: Donabedian model: structure, process, outcome
CMS quality reporting programs (Hospital Compare, MIPS)
Clinical quality measures (CQMs) and eCQMs
HEDIS measures for managed care
Six Sigma and Lean in healthcare quality improvement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of CMS Hospital Compare website and analysis of quality metrics for a sample hospital

review.value: Framework evaluating healthcare quality through structure (resources), process (care delivery), and outcomes (results)
Electronic Clinical Quality Measure: quality metrics computed directly from EHR data for automated reporting

learning_objectives: Analyze the impact of the Affordable Care Act on healthcare delivery
Understand current policy debates and reform proposals
Evaluate the role of informatics in policy implementation
readings: Jonas and Kovner's Health Care Delivery Ch. 14
Commonwealth Fund International Health System Comparison

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare policy directly shapes the systems informatics professionals build. Understanding the policy landscape ensures we design solutions that align with regulatory requirements and reform goals.
key_concepts: Affordable Care Act: key provisions and informatics requirements
Health insurance exchanges and enrollment systems
Medicaid expansion and its data implications
International comparisons: single-payer vs. multi-payer systems
Future reform proposals and informatics implications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of Healthcare.gov as a case study in health informatics system design, including its troubled launch and recovery
setup_requirements: Internet access for policy resource review

review.value: Individual mandate, insurance exchanges, Medicaid expansion, essential health benefits, pre-existing condition protections
Federal health insurance marketplace illustrating challenges of large-scale health IT implementation

questions.question: Explain the Triple Aim framework and provide a specific example of how a health informatics intervention could address each of its three goals.
questions.answer: The Triple Aim (IHI) pursues: (1) Better patient experience - a patient portal with easy access to records and secure messaging improves satisfaction; (2) Better population health - a population health dashboard identifying care gaps enables proactive outreach to patients overdue for screenings; (3) Lower per capita costs - predictive analytics identifying high-risk patients for care management reduces unnecessary ED visits and hospitalizations.

rubric.key: Correct identification of all three aims
Valid informatics example for each aim
Demonstrates understanding of how informatics supports value

questions.question: Compare fee-for-service and value-based payment models. What are the informatics requirements for a successful transition to value-based care?
questions.answer: Fee-for-service pays providers per service rendered, incentivizing volume. Value-based care ties reimbursement to quality outcomes and cost efficiency. The transition requires: robust quality measure calculation from EHR data, population health analytics platforms, care coordination tools for managing patient panels, risk stratification algorithms, and interoperable data exchange to track patients across settings.

rubric.key: Accurate comparison of FFS and VBC

questions.question: Describe the Patient-Centered Medical Home (PCMH) model and explain how at least three informatics tools support its core functions.
questions.answer: PCMH is a care delivery model where a primary care provider coordinates comprehensive care for each patient. Informatics tools supporting PCMH include: (1) EHR-integrated care gap alerts that flag patients needing preventive services; (2) secure patient portals enabling 24/7 communication and self-scheduling; (3) population health registries tracking chronic disease panels; (4) health information exchange enabling care coordination with specialists. These tools support PCMH's core functions of comprehensive care, coordinated care, and patient engagement.

rubric.key: Connection between tools and PCMH functions

questions.question: Using the Donabedian model, classify each of the following as a structure, process, or outcome measure and justify your classification: (a) 30-day readmission rate, (b) nurse-to-patient ratio, (c) percentage of patients screened for depression.
questions.answer: (a) 30-day readmission rate is an outcome measure because it reflects the end result of care - whether patients recovered sufficiently to avoid rehospitalization. (b) Nurse-to-patient ratio is a structure measure because it describes the organizational resources available to deliver care. (c) Percentage of patients screened for depression is a process measure because it reflects a specific care activity performed during the encounter.

major_assignments.title: Healthcare Delivery System Case Study
major_assignments.description: Select a healthcare organization (hospital system, health plan, or ACO) and produce a detailed case study analyzing its delivery model, financial structure, quality performance, and informatics infrastructure.
objectives: Apply healthcare delivery concepts to a real-world organization
Analyze financial and quality performance using publicly available data
Evaluate the role of information technology in the organization's strategy
deliverables: Written case study (10-12 pages, APA format)
Financial and quality data analysis appendix

criteria: Analysis of governance and strategic direction
Understanding of market and competitive context
Correct use of CMS quality data
Financial performance analysis from public filings
Thorough organizational analysis using authoritative sources
Rigorous data analysis with valid conclusions

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of healthcare delivery concepts and systems

class_participation.description: Active engagement in case study discussions and group activities

major_assignments.description: Healthcare delivery system case study and informatics proposal

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of healthcare system structure and financing

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all healthcare delivery and policy topics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction. Participation in case study discussions is required.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Extensions must be requested at least 48 hours before the deadline with valid justification.
course_policies.collaboration: Case study discussions are collaborative. Written assignments must be individual work unless designated as group projects.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper APA citations. Use of AI writing tools must be disclosed. Violations result in a zero on the assignment.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with internet access for in-class data analysis exercises. Access to CMS.gov, Hospital Compare, and course LMS.
